sentences_input.text: My father, and-- just the opposite, like my grandfather Enoch would open the window Shabbas morning, and-- and-- and read out loud from-- from the-- from the portion of the five books of Moses that was for this particular Saturday.
And so the whole community could see how well he knows it.
So it was very-- they were both wonderful people, but there was-- I could see the difference.
I was very close to my grandfather in Oswiecim, to Enoch, Wilhelm Enoch, because when he retired, he-- he raised pigeons.
And-- and when I came home from school, I used to help him with-- with the pigeons.

And he-- he knew, you know, the pigeons with the-- would sit down in-- in the middle of the little-- of the square in our building with food, and the pigeons would-- would come on over and sit down on his head and on the shoulders, and-- and he would feed them.
And he knew every pigeon, and he knew exactly how many he had.
It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's very unusual type of a hobby that he had.
And they both lived to be old people.
However, Not old enough because their life was cut down when the Germans sent them all to Auschwitz.
